phrasalVerbs.definition: To prove that something is true or correct.
examples: Subsequent research has borne out our initial hypothesis about climate change impacts.
The financial results bore out the analysts' predictions of a market downturn.
Her concerns were eventually borne out when the project failed to meet its objectives.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To use information or your experience of something to help you do something.
examples: The author draws on extensive field research conducted over two decades.
In developing our strategy, we drew on the expertise of specialists from multiple disciplines.
She drew on her experience as a diplomat to navigate the complex negotiations.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To add more details or information to a plan or idea.
examples: The committee will meet next week to flesh out the proposal before presenting it to the board.
Your concept is promising, but you'll need to flesh out the implementation details.
The researchers plan to flesh out their preliminary findings with additional case studies.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To solve problems or difficulties, especially small ones.
examples: We need to iron out a few logistical issues before we can proceed with the launch.
The negotiating teams worked through the night to iron out the remaining disagreements.
Once we've ironed out the technical glitches, the system should run smoothly.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make someone give clear details or a decision about something.
examples: It's been difficult to pin down the exact cause of the system failure.
I've been trying to pin him down on a delivery date, but he keeps being evasive.
The investigators worked tirelessly to pin down the facts of the case.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To complete something by doing one final thing.
examples: Let me round off this presentation with a summary of our key recommendations.
The conference was rounded off with a keynote address from the Nobel laureate.
We rounded off the project by publishing our findings in a peer-reviewed journal.

fillInExercises.sentence: The experimental data ___ the theoretical predictions made earlier.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bear out' means to prove or confirm that something is true.

fillInExercises.sentence: The professor ___ on decades of research experience when formulating his thesis.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Draw on' means to use knowledge or experience as a resource.

fillInExercises.sentence: The concept is sound, but we need to ___ the operational details.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Flesh out' means to add more details to develop an idea fully.

fillInExercises.sentence: The legal team spent weeks ___ out the contractual ambiguities.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Iron out' means to resolve problems or smooth out difficulties.

fillInExercises.sentence: I've been trying to ___ her ___ on a commitment date for months.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pin down' means to get someone to commit to specifics or a decision.

fillInExercises.sentence: The symposium was ___ off with a panel discussion featuring industry leaders.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Round off' means to complete something with a final element.

fillInExercises.sentence: The longitudinal study has ___ the correlation between these variables.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bear out' means to confirm or substantiate something with evidence.

fillInExercises.sentence: Before we proceed, we need to ___ out any remaining inconsistencies in the data.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Iron out' means to eliminate problems or resolve issues.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: The preliminary results seem promising. Have they [bear out] your original hypothesis?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Largely, yes. I've been [draw on] data from three separate longitudinal studies.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Excellent. You'll need to [flesh out] the methodology section before peer review.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Agreed. I also need to [iron out] some statistical inconsistencies in the appendix.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Have you been able to [pin down] a publication timeline with the journal editor?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Not yet, but I hope to [round off] the revisions by the end of the month.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: We need to [flesh out] the implementation roadmap before the stakeholder presentation.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: I can [draw on] similar projects I managed to provide realistic timelines.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Good. We also need to [iron out] the budget allocation discrepancies.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: I've been trying to [pin down] the finance department, but they're being evasive.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: The pilot phase has [bear out] our concerns about resource constraints.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Let's [round off] this meeting with clear action items for everyone.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Agreed. [draw on] lessons learned will be essential for phase two.
